Farn PolrrrcAr PnACrrcES CouMrssroN

.J.'
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0856
May 15,2003
Michael Ha;rison
Harrison & Harrison
23801Calabasas Road, Suite 1015
Calabasas, CA 9 13 02-l 59 5
Re: Your Request for Adviee
Our FiIe No. .4.-03-096
DearMr. Halrison:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions ofthe Political Reform Act (the uAct").l This advice is based upon
the facts as provided in your letter and in a telephone conversation with Commission
staff. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in its advice-giving capacity, (In
re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that this letter should not be construed
to evaluate any conduct that has aheady taken place. @egulation 18329(bX8XA).)
QUESTIONS
Do you have a conflict of interest in decisions regarding a law firm from which
you rent your office space on a month-to-month basis and to which you pay full
fair market value rent?
Are you prohibited from participating in decisions that might affectthe source of
a camp aign contributi on?
CONCLUSIONS
According to the facts you have presented, you rent office space from a law firm
on a month-to-month basis and you pay full fair market value rent. Consequently,
you will not have a conflict of interest in decisions regarding the law firm because
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise indicated.
1
2.
1


File No. 4-03-096
Page No. 2
you do not have any economic interest in the decision by virtue of your rental
agreement, as discussed below.
Because you were elected to the city council, because the amount of the campaign
contribution did not meet the $250 threshold for disqualification, and because the
contribution was made more than 12 months prior to the time of the decision, you
are not prohibited from participating in a decision that might affect the source of
the campaign contribution.
FACTS
You have subleased an office from a law firm now known as Green, De
Bortnowsky & Quintanilla ("the Firm") since October 1, 1998 on a month-to-month
basis. You are one of several subtenants, you are treated the same as the other
subtenants, and you pay fanr market value rent. You have no interest in the Firm and
receive no special accommodations or incentives, financial or otherwise.
On March 6,2001, you were elected as a member of the Calabasas City Council,
and you are now serving as mayor pro tem.
A member of the Firm contributed approximately $200.00 to your election
campaign lr;-2001.
On April 30, 2003 , the city council approved a request for proposals for city
attorney. The Firm intends to respond to that request. You want to know if you may
participate in the debate and consideration and vote when the proposals for city attorney
are presented to the city council.
ANALYSIS
A. Section 87100
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official at any level of state or local
government from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. The Commission has adopted the following eight-step analysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualifuing conflict of interest in a given governmental
decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).) Please note that each factor in the eight-step
analysis must be found for a conflict of interest to exist. If you determine you potentially
have a conflict of interest based on the steps below, the Act prohibits you from making,
participating in making, or influencing governmental decisions affecting your economic
interests. (Section 87 I 03.)
Steps One and Two: Is the individual a "public official" within the meaning of the Act
and will the public official be "making," "participating in making," or "using or
2


File No. 4-03-096
Page No. 3
attempting to use his or her official position to influence" a governmental decision?
(Sections 87 I 00, 82048 and 87 I 03 ; regulation 1 8700.)
Step Three: Identifr the public official's economic interests. (Regulation 18703.)
Step Four: For each of the public official's economic interests, determine whether that
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision which the public
official will be making, particrpating in making, or using or attempting to use his or her
official position to influence. (Regulation 18704.)
Step Five: Determine the applicable materiality standard for each economic interest,
based upon the degree of involvement determined pursuant to regulation 18704.
(Regulation 18705.)
Step Six: Determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision
will have amateial financial effect (as defined in regulation 18705) on each economic
interest identified pursuant to regulation 18703. (Regulation 18706.) If it is not
reasonably foreseeable that there will be amateial financial effect on any of the public
official's economic interests, the public official does not have a conflict of interest within
the meaning of the Act.
Step Seven: Determine if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect is distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally. If the reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, the public official does not have a conflict of interest within the
meaning of the Act. If the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the public
official's economic interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, the
public official has a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Act. (Regulation
18707.)
Step Eight: Determine if the public official's participation is legally required despite the
conflict of interest. (Regulation I 8708.)
The first two steps of the analysis are not at issue in your letter. As a member of
the Calabasas City Council, you are a "member, officer, employee or consultant of a state
or local government agency." consequently, you are a public official, and you are
subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048; regulation
18701.) You will be "making" a governmental decision when you, as a member of the
city council, vote on amatter, obligate or commit your agency to any course of action or
enter into any contractual agreement on behalf of your agency. (Regulation 18702.1.)
In this case, we need not consider all eight steps. Your question can be resolved
atthe third step in the conflict-of-interest analysis, which identifies the public official's
economic interests. A public official has a disqualiffing financial interest in a decision if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,


File No. A-03-096
Page No. 4
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on the official's
economic interests. Your facts suggest that you have the following economic interests:
a
a
Business Enttty: A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in
which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section
87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, parhrer,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation
18703.1(b));
Real Property: Apublic official has an economic interest in real property in which
he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b);
regulation 18703.2);
Sources of Income: A public official has an economic interest in any source of
income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12
months prior to the decision (section 87103(c); regulation lS7O3.3);
o
Source of Gifts: A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to
him or her if the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03 (e); regulation 187 03.4);
o Personal Finances: A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances and those of his orher immediate family. A govemmental decision will have
an effect on this economic interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing
or decreasing.
Real Property: Yottrent your office on a month-to-month basis. Under the Act, an
"interest in real property" includes a leasehold interest in real property within the
official's jurisdiction. (Section 82033.) However, the terms "interest in real property" and
"leasehold interest" as used in the Act do not include the interest of a tenant in a periodic
tenancy of one month or less. (Regulation 15233.) Therefore, you do not have areal
property interest in your office space, and you do not have an economic interest in your
office space for conflict-of-interest purposes. (Section 87103(b).)
Income and gifts: The definition of "income" includes loans and forgiveness of
indebtedness. (Section 82030.) If the Firm forgave part of the rent owed on the office,
the Firm would be a source of income. Further, section 82028 defines "gift" to include a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made
in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official
status. Thus, if you received some special discount in the rent paid for your office, the
Firm would be a source of gifts. However, you noted that the rent you are currently
paying is the same amount as the Firm's other subtenants are paying and is of fair market
value. Thus, the Firm does not appear to be a source of income or a gift.


File No. 4-03-096
Page No. 5
PersonalJinancial effect: Please note that although the month-to-month rental is not
areal property interest within the meaning of the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, you
could have a conflict of interest in any govemmental decision that would foreseeably 
-
affect the annual rental value of this lease by $250 or more, which would constifute a
material financial effect on your personal finances. (Regulation 1g705.5.)2
B. Section 84308
The Act prohibits an officer of an agency from making,pafiicipating in the
making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision in a proceeding if the officer has received a contribution of more than $250
within the preceding 12 months from a party or participant in the proceeding. (Section
84308(c).) However, this section does not apply to local governmental agencies whose
members are directly elected by the voters, e.g., the city council. (Section 8a308(a)(3).)
Furthermore, the campaign contribution was made more than 12 months preceding the 
-
decision and did not reach the $250 threshold required for disqualification.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
K.!.d *, " L^.J,t &,qdd
By: Kelly L. Winsor
Legal Analyst, L e gal Division
LMM:KLWjg
Enclosure
I:\Adviceltrs\03-096
2 You have notprovided sufficient facts for us to advise regarding the other sources of income or your
business. Please note that these may also be a basis for a conflict of interest, depending on the facis. We
have enclosed the pamphlet "Can I Vote?" for your information. You should contact ui for further advice
if you believe these other interests may be materially affected.

you rent your office space on a month-to-month basis and to which you pay full
fair market value rent?
Are you prohibited from participating in decisions that might affectthe source of
a camp aign contributi on?

CONCLUSION: According to the facts you have presented, you rent office space from a law firm
on a month-to-month basis and you pay full fair market value rent. Consequently,
you will not have a conflict of interest in decisions regarding the law firm because
1

you do not have any economic interest in the decision by virtue of your rental
agreement, as discussed below.
Because you were elected to the city council, because the amount of the campaign
contribution did not meet the $250 threshold for disqualification, and because the
contribution was made more than 12 months prior to the time of the decision, you
are not prohibited from participating in a decision that might affect the source of
the campaign contribution.
